title: Festuca johnsonii
intro: Festuca johnsonii, also known as Johnson's fescue, is a perennial grass native to the mountainous regions of North America. This plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including reducing inflammation and improving respiratory health, due to its potential health benefits in treating conditions such as bronchitis and asthma. The bioactive constituents of Festuca johnsonii include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and coumarins, which are responsible for its pharmacological properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Johnson's fescue include infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which can be used to support respiratory health and relieve symptoms of coughs and colds.
taxonomy: Festuca johnsonii is a species of grass in the family Poaceae. It belongs to the genus Festuca, which comprises over 300 species of grasses. Festuca johnsonii is further classified as a perennial or annual grass, depending on the specific growing conditions. The plant is native to New Zealand and is often found in grasslands and mountainous regions. It is a member of the subgenus Festuca, which contains many species of temperate grasses.
